41.problem_description: Compute the sum of the first three elements in an integer array and return the total.

kcs.name: Array element access by index
kcs.reasoning: Solutions directly access array elements using indices (nums[0], nums[1], nums[2]) to retrieve specific values.

kcs.name: Fixed-count loop iteration
kcs.reasoning: Index-based solutions use a loop boundary of i < 3 to iterate exactly 3 times regardless of array length.

kcs.name: Accumulator variable for summation
kcs.reasoning: Multiple solutions initialize a sum variable and incrementally add each element to compute the total.

kcs.name: Direct arithmetic expression evaluation
kcs.reasoning: Some solutions avoid loops entirely, instead using chained addition operators to compute the sum in a single expression.

kcs.name: Integer addition operator chaining
kcs.reasoning: Solutions combine multiple addition operations sequentially to sum three separate values into one result.

kcs.name: Method return with computed value
kcs.reasoning: All solutions return the computed integer sum, requiring proper non-void return type and return statement.

43.problem_description: Extract and return a front piece of an array: the first two elements if available, otherwise the entire array (or empty array if input is empty).

kcs.name: Conditional logic based on array length
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use if/else chains to branch on nums.length, checking thresholds of 0, 1, and 2 to determine output size.

kcs.name: Array instantiation with specified size
kcs.reasoning: Solutions create new arrays using 'new int[size]' with dynamically determined size (0, 1, or 2) based on input length.

kcs.name: Array element access and assignment
kcs.reasoning: Solutions populate new arrays by reading from input using nums[i] and writing to output using newArray[i] = nums[i].

kcs.name: Array length property usage
kcs.reasoning: All solutions access the .length property to determine input array size for conditional branching and loop bounds.

kcs.name: Method return type array declaration
kcs.reasoning: The method signature declares int[] return type, requiring proper construction and return of an array object.

kcs.name: Boundary-aware array slicing
kcs.reasoning: Solutions correctly handle edge cases (empty, single-element, multi-element) to extract the appropriate front portion without index out-of-bounds errors.

44.problem_description: Count how many elements in an integer array are even numbers and return the total count.

kcs.name: Array traversal with index or iterator
kcs.reasoning: All solutions iterate through every element using either index-based for loops or enhanced for-each loops to examine all array elements.

kcs.name: Modulo operator for divisibility testing
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use the % 2 == 0 pattern to determine whether each number is divisible by 2, identifying even integers.

kcs.name: Conditional counter increment pattern
kcs.reasoning: Each solution initializes a counter to zero and increments it only when the evenness condition is true, accumulating the count.

kcs.name: If statement for selective execution
kcs.reasoning: All solutions wrap the counter increment in an if block to execute the increment only for even numbers.

kcs.name: Method return of computed integer
kcs.reasoning: The method must return the final count as an int, requiring proper return statement and type consistency.

46.problem_description: Determine whether a given value appears in every consecutive pair of elements in an array (i.e., no two adjacent elements can both differ from the target value).

kcs.name: Array traversal with consecutive pair indexing
kcs.reasoning: All solutions iterate from 0 to length-2, accessing both nums[i] and nums[i+1] to examine adjacent element pairs.

kcs.name: Logical AND operator in conditionals
kcs.reasoning: The core logic uses && to combine two inequality checks, requiring both nums[i] != val AND nums[i+1] != val to trigger the violation case.

kcs.name: Loop boundary with length adjustment
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use i < nums.length - 1 to prevent array index out-of-bounds when accessing the next element at position i+1.

kcs.name: Early return for boolean negation
kcs.reasoning: Solutions immediately return false upon detecting a violation, then return true after loop completion if no violation was found.

kcs.name: Boolean method return type
kcs.reasoning: The method must declare a boolean return type and return true or false based on whether the property holds for the entire array.

kcs.name: Negation logic in violation detection
kcs.reasoning: Solutions recognize that the violation occurs when NEITHER element equals val (both are not equal), rather than checking for the positive case.

49.problem_description: Sum all elements in an array, but skip any element that is 13 and also skip the element immediately following a 13.

kcs.name: Array element filtering with conditions
kcs.reasoning: All solutions selectively include/exclude elements based on whether they equal 13, requiring conditional logic within the loop.

kcs.name: Loop counter manipulation in iteration
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 2, 4, and 5 increment the loop counter (i++) when encountering 13 to skip the next element automatically.

kcs.name: Accumulator variable pattern
kcs.reasoning: Every solution initializes and incrementally updates a sum variable to compute the total.

kcs.name: Checking adjacent array elements
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 1 and 3 access nums[i-1] to examine the previous element, requiring careful index boundary management.

kcs.name: Logical operators for compound conditions
kcs.reasoning: Solutions use || and && to combine multiple element checks (current element is 13, previous element is 13) into single conditional expressions.

kcs.name: Array length boundary validation
kcs.reasoning: Solutions properly handle edge cases like empty arrays or the first element by checking array length or loop index bounds.

67.problem_description: Search an array for two consecutive or nearby 7s and return true if found, false otherwise.

kcs.name: Array traversal with controlled loop bounds
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use for-loops with length adjustments (nums.length - 1 or nums.length - 2) to prevent index out-of-bounds when accessing ahead elements.

kcs.name: Nested conditional logic for pattern matching
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use nested if statements to first detect a 7, then check if adjacent or nearby positions contain another 7.

kcs.name: Array index offset access patterns
kcs.reasoning: Solutions check positions at i+1 and i+2 relative to current index, requiring safe boundary validation before accessing.

kcs.name: Early exit with return statement
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 1-4 use early return when pattern is found to immediately exit; Solution 5 uses break with a boolean flag, both demonstrating control flow optimization.

kcs.name: Boolean method return type
kcs.reasoning: The method signature returns boolean, requiring correct return statements with true/false literals.

kcs.name: Logical operators in compound conditions
kcs.reasoning: Boundary checks use logical AND (&&) to combine range validation with element access, avoiding index errors.

104.problem_description: Replace each zero in an array with the largest odd number found at or after that position, returning the modified array.

kcs.name: Array traversal with conditional modification
kcs.reasoning: All solutions iterate through the array and selectively modify elements when a zero is found.

kcs.name: Nested loop bounded search pattern
kcs.reasoning: Solutions use inner loops to search within a restricted range (from current or specific position to end) for matching elements.

kcs.name: Modulo operator for odd/even detection
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use the modulo operator (% 2 == 1) to identify odd numbers, or delegate this check to a helper method.

kcs.name: Maximum accumulator with comparison
kcs.reasoning: Each solution initializes an accumulator variable and updates it only when finding a larger odd number using a comparison operator.

kcs.name: In-place array mutation and return
kcs.reasoning: Solutions modify array elements directly and return the reference to the modified array without creating a copy.

kcs.name: Helper method extraction for logic
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 4 and 5 demonstrate extracting search or comparison logic into separate methods to improve readability and reusability.

106.problem_description: Determine if the first n elements of an array match the last n elements in order.

kcs.name: Dual pointer indexing strategy
kcs.reasoning: Solutions establish correspondence between prefix and suffix positions using two independently-managed indices starting from opposite array ends.

kcs.name: Array bounds calculation for suffix
kcs.reasoning: All solutions correctly compute the starting position of the suffix section as nums.length - n to avoid index out-of-bounds errors.

kcs.name: Element-by-element equality comparison
kcs.reasoning: Each solution compares pairs of elements from prefix and suffix positions using == or != operators within the loop.

kcs.name: Early exit pattern on condition failure
kcs.reasoning: Solutions use return false immediately upon detecting a mismatch, or accumulate results for later comparison.

kcs.name: Loop iteration with parameter-driven bounds
kcs.reasoning: The loop executes exactly n times, controlled by the parameter, to compare exactly n pairs from each end.

kcs.name: Boolean predicate method return
kcs.reasoning: The method returns a boolean value indicating whether the entire condition (matching ends) is satisfied.

107.problem_description: Count the number of distinct clumps (runs of consecutive equal elements with length > 1) in an integer array.

kcs.name: State machine with boolean flag
kcs.reasoning: Solutions 2, 4, 5 use a boolean flag to track whether currently inside a clump, preventing duplicate counts of the same run.

kcs.name: Adjacent element comparison in loop
kcs.reasoning: All solutions compare consecutive array elements using nums[i] == nums[i+1] to detect when equal values occur consecutively.

kcs.name: Loop boundary adjustment for pairs
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use i < nums.length - 1 to safely access both nums[i] and nums[i+1] without index out-of-bounds errors.

kcs.name: Accumulator counter pattern
kcs.reasoning: Each solution initializes a counter variable and increments it exactly once per clump detected.

kcs.name: Clump transition detection logic
kcs.reasoning: Solutions must identify the start of a clump (first consecutive pair) while skipping interior elements, requiring conditional logic to detect transitions.

kcs.name: Array traversal with indexed access
kcs.reasoning: All solutions use for or while loops with explicit index variables to iterate through the array.

108.problem_description: Fix an array so that every 4 is immediately followed by a 5 by finding orphan 5s (not preceded by 4) and swapping them into correct positions.

kcs.name: Nested loop array search pattern
kcs.reasoning: Solutions use an outer loop to scan for problematic 4s and an inner loop to locate matching orphan 5s, requiring careful index management across nested scopes.

kcs.name: Compound conditional with multiple criteria
kcs.reasoning: All solutions check both that a 4 exists (nums[i] == 4) and that it lacks a following 5 (nums[i+1] != 5) in a single if condition.

kcs.name: Finding elements by property with constraints
kcs.reasoning: The inner loop searches for a 5 that satisfies a constraint: either at index 0 or not preceded by a 4, requiring logical AND/OR combination of conditions.

kcs.name: Array element swapping via reassignment
kcs.reasoning: Solutions swap values by assigning the misplaced element to the orphan position and placing 5 after the 4, modifying the array in-place.

kcs.name: Index variable independence in nested loops
kcs.reasoning: Outer and inner loop indices must operate independently; the inner index must reset or continue across multiple outer iterations without interfering with outer control flow.

kcs.name: Negation of complex boolean expressions
kcs.reasoning: The loop condition negates a compound boolean (!(A && B)) requiring correct operator precedence and De Morgan's law understanding.

kcs.name: Boundary checking for array access safety
kcs.reasoning: Solutions prevent index out-of-bounds by using loop conditions like i < nums.length - 1 and checking j-1 existence before accessing nums[j-1].
